verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
इमं विवस्वते योगं प्रोक्तवानहमव्ययम् ।
विवस्वान्मनवे प्राह मनुरिक्ष्वाकवेऽब्रवीत् ॥ १ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
imaṁ vivasvate yogaṁ proktavān aham avyayam |
vivasvān manave prāha manur ikṣvākave 'bravīt || 1 ||
verses.literal_meaning: The Lord said: I taught this imperishable yoga to Vivasvān; Vivasvān spoke it to Manu; Manu spoke it to Ikṣvāku.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna opens chapter 4 by establishing the antiquity of the teaching through paramparā (lineage). The yoga he is teaching Arjuna is the same imperishable (avyaya) yoga he originally taught to Vivasvān (the sun-god), who passed it to Manu (the progenitor of humankind), who passed it to Ikṣvāku (founder of the solar dynasty). The stake: the teaching's authority is not novel but lineaged. The verse opens the chapter's first thread (lineage and divine action).
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna establishing that yoga was first imparted to Vivasvān at the beginning of creation in order to infuse strength into the kṣatriyas (warrior-rulers) so they could protect the brāhmaṇas and uphold the world. The yoga is both imperishable and originally given for the kṣatriya's purpose of protecting dharma.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja groups verses 4.1-4.2 and reads them as Krishna saying: this karma yoga should not be taken as something taught now merely to encourage Arjuna to fight. Krishna originally taught this yoga to Vivasvān at the beginning of Manu's age as the means by which all beings attain release (the supreme end). Vivasvān taught Manu, Manu taught Ikṣvāku, and royal sages knew this yoga across generations.

positions.rendering: this imperishable science of yoga (the science of one's relationship to the Supreme)
the imperishable yoga, originally taught to give strength to kṣatriyas for protecting dharma
karma yoga as the means for all beings to attain release / the supreme end

disagreements.explanation: Prabhupada renders 'yoga' as 'the science of one's relationship to the Supreme' — a Gaudiya devotional emphasis. Shankara reads the yoga as originally aimed at kṣatriyas for the protection of dharma (a kṣatriya-svadharma emphasis consistent with Advaita's social-order reading). Ramanuja reads the yoga as karma-yoga aimed at universal release — a Vishishtadvaita reading that emphasises the soteriological scope. All three agree the yoga is avyaya (imperishable) and was transmitted by lineage; they differ on whom the yoga is primarily aimed at.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
एवं परम्पराप्राप्तमिमं राजर्षयो विदुः ।
स कालेनेह महता योगो नष्टः परन्तप ॥ २ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
evaṁ paramparā-prāptam imaṁ rājarṣayo viduḥ |
sa kāleneha mahatā yogo naṣṭaḥ paran-tapa || 2 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Thus received in lineage, the royal sages knew this yoga; over a long time, Arjuna, that yoga was lost in this world.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The lineage of paramparā is acknowledged: royal sages preserved the yoga across generations. But Krishna names a fact that bears doctrinal weight — the yoga was lost (naṣṭaḥ) over time. This sets up 4.3, where Krishna explains why he is re-imparting it now. The verse honors the principle that even authentic transmission decays without sustained practice and worthy successors.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads naṣṭaḥ as the yoga having become disrupted because the strength of those receiving it had weakened — those without self-control received it, leading to its decline. The yoga itself is not destroyed in essence; the chain of competent receivers broke.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as continuous with 4.1: this yoga was passed through royal sages who knew it, but in the long course of time it was lost — meaning unrealised, no longer practiced as the path to liberation it was given as. The verse establishes the necessity of Krishna's re-instruction.

positions.rendering: lost (the chain was broken)
lost in the sense that competent receivers (those with self-control) became scarce
lost in the sense of unrealised — the yoga as a path to liberation ceased to be lived

disagreements.explanation: All three agree the yoga was 'lost' but locate the loss differently. Prabhupada flattens to a broken transmission chain. Shankara emphasises the receiver-side — when those receiving the yoga lacked self-control, the yoga's transmission failed. Ramanuja emphasises the praxis-side — the yoga ceased to be lived as a soteriological path. The Advaita reading is more focused on epistemic competence; the Vishishtadvaita reading is more focused on lived practice.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
स एवायं मया तेऽद्य योगः प्रोक्तः पुरातनः ।
भक्तोऽसि मे सखा चेति रहस्यं ह्येतदुत्तमम् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
sa evāyaṁ mayā te 'dya yogaḥ proktaḥ purātanaḥ |
bhakto 'si me sakhā ceti rahasyaṁ hy etad uttamam || 3 ||
verses.literal_meaning: That very same ancient yoga is today told by me to you; you are my devotee and friend, and this is the supreme secret.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna re-discloses the yoga to Arjuna and grounds the disclosure in personal relation: bhakta (devotee) and sakhā (friend). The verse names the teaching as rahasyam uttamam — the supreme secret — meaning the deepest teaching. The combination of relation (devotee + friend) and content (supreme secret) establishes the conditions of authentic transmission: the teaching's depth requires a worthy receiver, characterised by both reverence and intimacy.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna explaining why he re-imparts the yoga to Arjuna: because Arjuna is both bhakta and sakhā, he is a fit receiver. The relation qualifies Arjuna; the relation is what makes possible the disclosure of rahasyam uttamam.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as emphasising that the relationship of devotion and friendship is itself the qualification — Arjuna's reverence and intimacy combined make him capable of receiving the highest secret. The teaching is given on the basis of the relation, not on the basis of merit alone.

positions.rendering: the supreme secret (of the science of yoga)
the highest secret — the yoga whose depth makes it secret to those without the qualifying relation
the supreme mystery accessible through the qualifying relation of devotee + friend

disagreements.explanation: All three agree this is the highest teaching. They differ on what makes it 'secret': for Prabhupada it is the depth of the science itself; for Shankara it is the relational qualification of the receiver; for Ramanuja the relation IS the access. The Vishishtadvaita reading binds the secret-ness more tightly to bhakti.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच
अपरं भवतो जन्म परं जन्म विवस्वतः ।
कथमेतद्विजानीयां त्वमादौ प्रोक्तवानिति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
arjuna uvāca
aparaṁ bhavato janma paraṁ janma vivasvataḥ |
katham etad vijānīyāṁ tvam ādau proktavān iti || 4 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Arjuna said: Your birth was later, Vivasvān's was earlier; how should I understand that you taught it in the beginning?
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna asks the obvious chronological question: Krishna's birth in this lifetime is recent; Vivasvān is from a remote epoch. How can the same person have taught Vivasvān? The question is sincere, not skeptical — it sets up Krishna's answer at 4.5 about identity-across-births. This is Arjuna's only major interruption in the first ten verses of the chapter.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Arjuna raising the apparent contradiction between Krishna's recent birth and the antiquity of the original teaching to Vivasvān. The question is necessary in order to elicit Krishna's doctrine of recurring births and divine knowledge spanning them.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as Arjuna's doubt arising specifically because Krishna seems to be a normal human born in this age, whereas the teaching is ancient. The question is what creates space for Krishna to declare in 4.5-4.9 that he is not bound by ordinary birth.

positions.rendering: How am I to understand that in the beginning You instructed this science?
Arjuna's sincere question, raised in order that the doctrine of Krishna's transcendental nature may be declared
Arjuna's doubt is honest and necessary; it is the cue for Krishna's revelation of his recurring incarnations

disagreements.explanation: All three commentators agree Arjuna's question is genuine, not adversarial. Both classical commentators read it as a teaching device — the question's role is to make space for the avatar doctrine that follows. None of them read Arjuna as challenging Krishna's authority.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
बहूनि मे व्यतीतानि जन्मानि तव चार्जुन ।
तान्यहं वेद सर्वाणि न त्वं वेत्थ परन्तप ॥ ५ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
bahūni me vyatītāni janmāni tava cārjuna |
tāny ahaṁ veda sarvāṇi na tvaṁ vettha paran-tapa || 5 ||
verses.literal_meaning: The Lord said: Many of my births have passed, and yours too, Arjuna; I know all of them, you do not, scorcher of foes.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna answers Arjuna's chronological question by declaring an asymmetry: both have had many births, but only Krishna remembers them. The verse sets up the avatar doctrine without yet stating it: the difference between Krishna and Arjuna is not that one has been born and the other has not; it is that one knows all his births and the other does not. The verse foregrounds knowledge as the discriminator.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna establishing that ordinary jīva consciousness loses awareness across births, whereas the Lord retains full knowledge of his successive births because his nature is unobscured by māyā. The asymmetry lies in unobstructed self-knowledge.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as Krishna's claim that he is sarvajña (all-knowing) — knowing his own previous births and those of all beings — while Arjuna, being an embodied jīva, does not have this knowledge. The verse establishes Krishna's omniscience without yet naming the avatar mechanism.

positions.rendering: Many, many births both you and I have passed. I can remember all of them, but you cannot, O subduer of the enemy!
Krishna retains unobscured self-knowledge across births; Arjuna's jīva consciousness is veiled by māyā and cannot recall
Krishna is sarvajña (omniscient over all beings' births); Arjuna's knowledge is bounded by jīva-level cognition

disagreements.explanation: Prabhupada reads 'veda' simply as 'remember.' Shankara emphasises the metaphysical mechanism (māyā veils the jīva but not the Lord). Ramanuja emphasises the scope (Krishna knows all beings' births, not just his own). The three readings together preserve the asymmetry the verse establishes without flattening it into ordinary memory.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
अजोऽपि सन्नव्ययात्मा भूतानामीश्वरोऽपि सन् ।
प्रकृतिं स्वामधिष्ठाय सम्भवाम्यात्ममायया ॥ ६ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
ajo 'pi sann avyayātmā bhūtānām īśvaro 'pi san |
prakṛtiṁ svām adhiṣṭhāya sambhavāmy ātma-māyayā || 6 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Though unborn and imperishable in nature, though Lord of beings, presiding over my own prakṛti, I come into being through my own māyā.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna states the avatar doctrine for the first time. He is unborn (aja) and imperishable (avyayātmā), the Lord (īśvara) of beings — yet, presiding over his own prakṛti (creative power), he manifests through his own māyā (yogic self-disposing power). The verse holds two simultaneous claims: ontologically Krishna does not undergo birth in the way embodied beings do; phenomenally he appears to. The mechanism is ātma-māyā — his own creative power, not external constraint.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna's statement of the apparent paradox: the unborn and imperishable Lord appears to take birth, but only by his own māyā. Māyā here is the divine creative power, not illusion in the negative sense — Krishna's appearance is real-seeming but not bound by the causal chain that produces ordinary births.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as central: the Lord, who is by nature unborn and imperishable and the controller of all beings, presides over his own prakṛti (his own divine body, śuddha-sattva nature) and incarnates through his own divine will (ātma-māyā as divine self-disposing). The avatar's body is real and divine, not illusory.

positions.rendering: I appear in My original transcendental form (through internal potency)
By my own māyā (divine creative power); the appearance is not bound by ordinary causation
Through divine self-disposing — the avatar body is genuinely divine (śuddha-sattva), the result of svarūpa-saṅkalpa

disagreements.explanation: The major fault-line: Shankara permits a reading where the avatar's body is māyā in a sense closer to 'apparent form'; Ramanuja insists the avatar's body is real and divine, not māyic in any reducing sense. Prabhupada follows the Vishishtadvaita-Gaudiya reading. This is one of the most theologically loaded verses in the chapter.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत ।
अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम् ॥ ७ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata |
abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṁ sṛjāmy aham || 7 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Whenever there is decline of dharma, Bharata, and rise of adharma, then I manifest myself.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The most famous verse of chapter 4. Krishna names the cyclical pattern: when dharma declines (glāniḥ) and adharma rises, he manifests (sṛjāmi ātmānam). The verb sṛjāmi is striking — usually 'I create,' here applied reflexively (I manifest myself). Prabhupada explicitly notes that sṛjāmi here cannot mean 'create' since the Lord's form is eternal; it means 'manifest' in the sense of self-disposing appearance. The verse is the doctrinal centerpiece of the avatar block.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads dharma here as 'virtue consisting of the duties of castes and stages of life of living beings, which are the means to achieving prosperity and Liberation.' When that declines and adharma rises, the Lord manifests himself through māyā. The mechanism is consistent with 4.6 — appearance, not origination.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja explicitly says: 'There is no restriction as to the time of My birth.' Whenever the Dharma taught by the Vedas — the dharma of the four stations and stages of life — declines and adharma increases, the Lord by his own will incarnates in the manner stated (4.6). The incarnation is regular, will-governed, real-bodied.

positions.rendering: religious practice
the duties of castes and stages of life, which are means to prosperity and Liberation
the Dharma taught by the Vedas, observed according to the arrangements of the four stations and four stages

disagreements.explanation: All three commentators read 'dharma' as the structured order of duties prescribed for varṇas (stations) and āśramas (stages). Modern translations often flatten dharma to 'religion' or 'righteousness' but the classical reading is more specific — the structured sociocultural-soteriological order. The engineering analog must respect this scope: not 'morality in general' but 'the operational discipline of a structured order'.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम् ।
धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे ॥ ८ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṁ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām |
dharma-saṁsthāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge || 8 ||

anvaya.meaning: for the purpose of firm establishment
I am born / I come forth

verses.literal_meaning: For the protection of the good, the destruction of the wicked, and the firm establishment of dharma — I come forth age after age.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The threefold purpose of the avatar: (1) paritrāṇāya sādhūnām — protection of the virtuous, (2) vināśāya duṣkṛtām — destruction of the wicked, (3) dharma-saṁsthāpanārtha — re-establishment of dharma. The verse is the most quoted in popular religious culture. Yuge yuge marks recurrence: this is not a one-time intervention but a cyclical pattern across cosmic ages.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as the explicit purpose-statement of incarnation: protection of the dharma-keepers, destruction of the dharma-violators, and the firm reinstatement of dharma itself. All three are aimed at preserving the conditions under which liberation remains accessible.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as expanding 4.7's mechanism into purpose. The Lord's incarnation is not punitive in any narrow sense; the destruction of the wicked is itself a deliverance for them (their adharma is dissolved by encounter with the divine), and the protection of the saintly is the preservation of those whose lives sustain the dharma. The repeated yuge yuge marks the regularity, not the rarity.

positions.rendering: to annihilate the miscreants
destruction of the dharma-violators, aimed at restoring the conditions for liberation
the destruction is itself a deliverance — encounter with the divine dissolves the duṣkṛta's adharma

disagreements.explanation: The fault-line is whether 'destruction' is read narrowly (punishment) or broadly (transformation by divine encounter). Ramanuja's compassionate reading is distinctive: the wicked are not merely punished, they are released. The Vishishtadvaita reading is theologically closer to a soteriology of grace. Engineering layers must not flatten this verse into 'remove bad actors'; the Sanskrit allows multiple readings.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
जन्म कर्म च मे दिव्यमेवं यो वेत्ति तत्त्वतः ।
त्यक्त्वा देहं पुनर्जन्म नैति मामेति सोऽर्जुन ॥ ९ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
janma karma ca me divyam evaṁ yo vetti tattvataḥ |
tyaktvā dehaṁ punar janma naiti mām eti so 'rjuna || 9 ||
verses.literal_meaning: He who knows in truth my birth and action as divine — leaving the body, he does not come to birth again; he comes to me, Arjuna.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse names the soteriological consequence of right knowledge of the avatar. To know Krishna's birth and action as divyam (divine, not ordinary) is liberating: such a knower, on dropping the body, does not return to saṁsāra but reaches Krishna. The verse links knowledge (vetti tattvataḥ) to the transcending of rebirth — the chapter's first explicit linking of jñāna to mokṣa.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as naming the fruit of correct knowledge of the avatar's nature: such a knower is freed from the cycle of rebirth and reaches the Lord. The crucial qualifier is tattvataḥ — knowledge in essence, not nominal recognition.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as central to bhakti soteriology: knowing the Lord's birth and action as divine (not ordinary, not māyic-in-the-illusory-sense) generates the relation of devotion that, at death, brings the devotee to the Lord. The fruit is union with Krishna, not impersonal liberation.

positions.rendering: attains My eternal abode (Goloka, the spiritual world)
reaches Brahman / the Self that the Lord is
reaches the Lord himself in personal union (sāyujya)

disagreements.explanation: The classic Advaita-Vishishtadvaita-Gaudiya difference on what 'reaching Krishna' is. For Shankara, mām eti is reaching the impersonal Self (Krishna's deepest identity is Brahman). For Ramanuja, it is sāyujya — personal union with the Lord. For Prabhupada, it is going to Goloka, the spiritual planet of Krishna's pastimes. The engineering layer should preserve the verse's claim (knowledge → liberation) without flattening to a sectarian reading.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
वीतरागभयक्रोधा मन्मया मामुपाश्रिताः ।
बहवो ज्ञानतपसा पूता मद्भावमागताः ॥ १० ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhā man-mayā mām upāśritāḥ |
bahavo jñāna-tapasā pūtā mad-bhāvam āgatāḥ || 10 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Freed from attachment, fear, and anger, absorbed in me, taking refuge in me — many, purified by the austerity of knowledge, have come to my state.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names the qualifying conditions for the result described in 4.9. The qualifying conditions are: freedom from rāga (attachment), bhaya (fear), and krodha (anger); manmayā (absorption in Krishna); upāśrita (taking refuge in Krishna); and purification by jñāna-tapas (the austerity of knowledge). The verse is empirical, not promissory: bahavaḥ (many) have already attained mad-bhāva (Krishna's state). The result is named, the conditions are named.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as naming the fruits of knowledge accompanied by the renunciation of the three psychological disturbances (rāga, bhaya, krodha) and the adoption of the Lord as refuge. Jñāna-tapas is the austerity OF knowledge — the discipline of holding right knowledge against the tendency to relapse into ignorance.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as describing the bhakta who has integrated devotion (manmayā), taken refuge (upāśrita), and been purified by the austerity of knowledge (jñāna-tapas) — and who, having done so, has actually reached mad-bhāva (the state of being like the Lord). The verse is reassurance through precedent: this is not theoretical, many have crossed.

positions.rendering: transcendental love for Me
my state — i.e., Brahman, the Self
the state of being like the Lord (sādharmya), not identity

disagreements.explanation: The standard difference: Shankara reads mad-bhāva as the impersonal Brahman-state; Ramanuja reads it as sādharmya (similarity-of-nature) without identity; Prabhupada reads it as transcendental love. All three preserve the soteriological force of the verse but differ on what 'reaching Krishna's state' actually means.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
ये यथा मां प्रपद्यन्ते तांस्तथैव भजाम्यहम् ।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः ॥ ११ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
ye yathā māṁ prapadyante tāṁs tathaiva bhajāmy aham |
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣyāḥ pārtha sarvaśaḥ || 11 ||
verses.literal_meaning: As those approach me, so I receive them; humans follow my path in all ways, Pārtha.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names a reciprocity-principle. Whatever shape one's approach takes, the Lord's reception matches that shape. Sarvaśaḥ — in all ways — humans follow Krishna's path; the path is one but the modes of approach are many. The verse is often quoted as the Gītā's most pluralistic statement.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna's claim that he reciprocates each devotee's mode of approach in the same mode — the seeker after liberation receives liberation, the seeker after worldly fruits receives those, etc. The path is the Lord's; the diversity is in the approach.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse strongly: the Lord, being impartial yet responsive, requites each devotee according to the mode of their approach — meaning the relation is mutual. The verse is not a flat 'all paths are equal' claim; it is a reciprocity claim, where the Lord's reception is shaped by the approach itself.

positions.rendering: As all surrender unto Me, I reward them accordingly
Each is received in the form of their approach (worldly seekers get worldly fruits; liberation-seekers get liberation)
The Lord's reciprocity is real and mode-shaped, not a flat path-equivalence

disagreements.explanation: Modern popular readings often flatten this verse to 'all paths are equal.' The classical reading is sharper: the Lord's response IS shaped by the approach. Worldly approach → worldly fruit; liberation-seeking approach → liberation. The diversity is honored, but the differential receptions are also honored.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
काङ्क्षन्तः कर्मणां सिद्धिं यजन्त इह देवताः ।
क्षिप्रं हि मानुषे लोके सिद्धिर्भवति कर्मजा ॥ १२ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
kāṅkṣantaḥ karmaṇāṁ siddhiṁ yajanta iha devatāḥ |
kṣipraṁ hi mānuṣe loke siddhir bhavati karma-jā || 12 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Those desiring success of actions worship the gods here; quickly indeed in the human world is success born of action.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna acknowledges that those seeking specific outcomes through ritual action do receive those outcomes — the gods (devatāḥ) reciprocate ritual offerings with worldly results, and these results come quickly in the human realm. The verse is empirical, not condemnatory: there is a real karma-pathway to specific fruits. The implicit contrast is with the deeper liberation-path described in 4.9-4.11.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna's acknowledgement that ritual worship of specific deities for specific results does produce those results, and does so quickly in the human world. The verse establishes the validity of karma-kāṇḍa (ritual action) at its own level while implicitly pointing beyond it.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as part of the path-equivalence claim of 4.11 made concrete: those who approach the gods seeking specific fruits do receive them. The verse honors the karma-mīmāṁsā tradition (Vedic ritualism) without denying that more comprehensive fruits await different approaches.

positions.rendering: success born of fruitive work / ritualistic activities
specific worldly results produced quickly by deity-directed ritual action
the empirical fact that karma-yajña does produce its specified fruits

disagreements.explanation: All three commentators read the verse as acknowledging that ritual worship genuinely produces its named fruits. The quietly important word is karma-jā — born of action. The fruit is a real consequence of the action, but it is the fruit-of-action level only; the verse implicitly contrasts with deeper paths.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
चातुर्वर्ण्यं मया सृष्टं गुणकर्मविभागशः ।
तस्य कर्तारमपि मां विद्ध्यकर्तारमव्ययम् ॥ १३ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
cātur-varṇyaṁ mayā sṛṣṭaṁ guṇa-karma-vibhāgaśaḥ |
tasya kartāram api māṁ viddhy akartāram avyayam || 13 ||
verses.literal_meaning: The four-fold typology was created by me according to the division of guṇa and karma; though I am its creator, know me as non-doer, unchanging.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The chapter's most doctrinally fraught verse. Krishna explicitly says cātur-varṇya was created BY GUṆA AND KARMA — by intrinsic disposition (guṇa) and acquired action (karma) — NOT by birth. The historical caste-by-birth reading is an extension; the verse itself says guṇa-karma. The second hemistich adds: though Krishna is the creator of the typology, he is also non-doer (akartā), unchanging (avyaya). Both halves of the verse are loaded — the first by the historical caste-system reading, the second by the metaphysical claim of agency-without-binding.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads guṇa-karma-vibhāgaśaḥ as: the four varṇas were created according to the division of guṇa (sattva, rajas, tamas) and the corresponding karma (action) appropriate to each. This is a typology by intrinsic and acquired qualities, not by birth-line. The akartā / avyaya clause is read in Advaita fashion: the Lord as Brahman is creator only at the empirical level; in essence, he is non-doer.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as the Lord's declaration that the four-fold ordering of human society reflects an underlying division by guṇa and karma — the Lord's creative ordering follows the intrinsic and acquired qualities of beings. The Lord is creator at the cosmic level but, by his nature as the unchanging substrate, is also akartā in the deeper sense.

positions.rendering: the four divisions of human society, according to the modes of material nature and the work attributed to them
the four varṇas, created according to the division of the three guṇas and the karma corresponding to each
the four-fold ordering, reflecting the underlying division by intrinsic guṇa and acquired karma

disagreements.explanation: What the verse explicitly says: typology by guṇa and karma. What the verse does NOT say: typology by birth. The historical caste-by-birth reading is a later extension, contested even within tradition. Engineering layers must honor what the verse explicitly says (typology by intrinsic disposition + acquired action) and not smuggle in the birth-based reading. This is a STRETCHED-tag candidate verse if the engineering layer cannot honor both halves cleanly.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
न मां कर्माणि लिम्पन्ति न मे कर्मफले स्पृहा ।
इति मां योऽभिजानाति कर्मभिर्न स बध्यते ॥ १४ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
na māṁ karmāṇi limpanti na me karma-phale spṛhā |
iti māṁ yo 'bhijānāti karmabhir na sa badhyate || 14 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Actions do not smear me; I have no longing for the fruits of action; whoever clearly knows me thus is not bound by actions.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names two facts: actions do not bind him (na limpanti), and he has no longing for fruits (na spṛhā). The verse then closes with the soteriological claim: whoever knows Krishna's mode of acting — engaged action without binding — is themselves freed from binding by their own actions. The verse models for the practitioner what right-action looks like: action without longing for fruits.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as the model: the Lord acts (creates the four varṇas, sustains the world) without being bound, because he has no spṛhā for fruits. The knower who recognises this mode of acting — and adopts it — is similarly unbound. Knowledge of the Lord's non-binding action becomes the practitioner's release.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as Krishna's self-revelation: he acts but is not bound because his action is performed without desire for fruits. The bhakta who knows this and acts in the same mode (without selfish desire, in service to the Lord) is also released from karmic binding.

positions.rendering: knows in truth
knows clearly enough to adopt the same mode of action
knows with the recognition that issues in devotion-shaped action

disagreements.explanation: All three read abhijānāti as more than nominal recognition — it is a knowledge that becomes practice. Shankara emphasises the cognitive-imitative aspect (knowing how the Lord acts → acting that way). Ramanuja emphasises the relational aspect (knowing the Lord → devotion-shaped action). The verse's force is in the practical consequence, not the propositional content.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
एवं ज्ञात्वा कृतं कर्म पूर्वैरपि मुमुक्षुभिः ।
कुरु कर्मैव तस्मात्त्वं पूर्वैः पूर्वतरं कृतम् ॥ १५ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
evaṁ jñātvā kṛtaṁ karma pūrvair api mumukṣubhiḥ |
kuru karmaiva tasmāt tvaṁ pūrvaiḥ pūrvataraṁ kṛtam || 15 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Knowing thus, action was performed by the ancients seeking liberation; therefore you also perform action, as performed by the ancients in earlier times.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna closes the avatar block by pointing back to the disciplic tradition: the ancients (pūrvaiḥ) who sought liberation (mumukṣubhiḥ) performed action knowing what 4.14 has just established — that right-action does not bind. The instruction is: act as they acted. The verse re-grounds the chapter's teaching in lineage, completing the arc opened at 4.1-4.3 (paramparā) and now expressed as a directive: do as the lineage did.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as the directive that follows from the doctrine: knowing right-action does not bind, perform action as the liberated ancients did. The verse explicitly forbids inaction-as-renunciation; karma is to be done, not abandoned, in the manner the ancients did.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as authoritative: the practice of karma-yoga in the manner the ancients (Vivasvān, Manu, Ikṣvāku, the rājarṣis) practiced it — engaged action without longing for fruits — is what Arjuna is being told to do. The lineage is the model; the action is the practice.

positions.rendering: as performed by the ancients in days of yore
as the liberation-seeking ancients performed it, knowing right-action does not bind
in the manner Vivasvān, Manu, and the royal sages practiced karma-yoga across generations

disagreements.explanation: All three read pūrvataram as anchoring the directive in lineage-practice. The verse closes the chapter's first major movement by connecting the avatar doctrine back to the original paramparā opening (4.1-4.3). The instruction is to do the practice the lineage already did.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
किं कर्म किमकर्मेति कवयोऽप्यत्र मोहिताः ।
तत्ते कर्म प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात् ॥ १६ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
kiṁ karma kim akarmeti kavayo 'py atra mohitāḥ |
tat te karma pravakṣyāmi yaj jñātvā mokṣyase 'śubhāt || 16 ||
verses.literal_meaning: What is action and what is inaction — even the wise are confused here; I shall declare to you that action knowing which you will be freed from the inauspicious.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna pivots into the action-in-inaction discussion. He acknowledges that even kavi (the wise, the seers) are confused about the distinction between karma and akarma. The implication: this is not a trivial dyad. Krishna will now declare the precise teaching that resolves the confusion. The verse sets up 4.17 (the threefold typology) and 4.18 (the headline seeing).
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna's preparatory statement: the matter of action vs. inaction is so subtle that even the seers are confused. The verse signals that the teaching that follows (4.18 in particular) is one that requires careful attention. The fruit (mokṣa from aśubha) is named to establish stakes.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as Krishna explicitly addressing the difficulty: distinguishing action from inaction is hard even for the wise. The verse establishes that what follows is doctrinally important — the teaching that releases from the inauspicious (the bondage of karma).

positions.rendering: Even the intelligent are bewildered in determining what is action and what is inaction
Even the seers (kavi) are confused — the matter is genuinely subtle
The wise themselves find this difficult; therefore Krishna declares it explicitly

disagreements.explanation: All three read mohitāḥ honestly as 'confused' — Krishna is not being rhetorical. The matter genuinely escapes ordinary discrimination. The verse's role is to motivate close attention to 4.17-4.18.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मणो ह्यपि बोद्धव्यं बोद्धव्यं च विकर्मणः ।
अकर्मणश्च बोद्धव्यं गहना कर्मणो गतिः ॥ १७ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
karmaṇo hy api boddhavyaṁ boddhavyaṁ ca vikarmaṇaḥ |
akarmaṇaś ca boddhavyaṁ gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ || 17 ||
verses.literal_meaning: The truth of action is to be known, and the truth of forbidden action, and the truth of inaction; the way of action is profound.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names a threefold typology: karma (right action), vikarma (forbidden action / wrong action), and akarma (inaction). All three must be understood (boddhavyam). The closing claim — gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ — names the depth: the way of action is profound, deep, hard to penetrate. The verse is the philosophical setup for the headline 4.18.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads vikarma as 'prohibited action' (action contrary to dharma) — the third term that complicates the simple action/inaction dyad. The threefold typology (right action, prohibited action, inaction) must all be discriminated. Gahanā gatiḥ signals: this is genuinely hard, not merely rhetorical difficulty.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the threefold typology as karma (Vedic-prescribed action), vikarma (action against the Veda), and akarma (the abstention from action which itself is a karma in a sense). All three require understanding. The way of action is deep because what looks like inaction may be karma, what looks like karma may be akarma — the surface does not disclose the doctrinal status.

positions.rendering: forbidden work
prohibited action / action contrary to dharma
action against the Veda's prescriptions

disagreements.explanation: All three read vikarma as 'wrong-action' — forbidden, prohibited, or against the Vedic order. The verse is uncontroversial on this point. The interesting term is akarma — what counts as 'inaction.' The classical commentators split on whether akarma is mere abstention or whether it is a positive non-doership state. 4.18 will resolve this.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मण्यकर्म यः पश्येदकर्मणि च कर्म यः ।
स बुद्धिमान्मनुष्येषु स युक्तः कृत्स्नकर्मकृत् ॥ १८ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ |
sa buddhimān manuṣyeṣu sa yuktaḥ kṛtsna-karma-kṛt || 18 ||
verses.literal_meaning: He who sees inaction in action and action in inaction — he is wise among humans; he is established in yoga, the doer of all action.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The chapter's headline verse and one of the most famous verses in the entire Gītā. Krishna names a single discriminative seeing in two directions: (1) seeing inaction (akarma) in apparent action (karma) — the actor who acts without doership; (2) seeing action (karma) in apparent inaction (akarma) — the apparent non-doer who is actually performing action (or whose 'inaction' is itself a positive karma). The one who sees both ways is buddhimān (wise) among humans, yukta (established in yoga), and — crucially — kṛtsna-karma-kṛt (the doer of all action). The verse rejects the equation of yoga with idleness: the right-seer is the most effective actor, not the least.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara famously reads this verse as resolving the action/inaction confusion through the doctrine of non-doership: in the actor who has realised the Self (which is non-acting), even visible action is metaphysically akarma — there is no doer. Conversely, in the ignorant person who abstains from physical action but whose mind is still filled with desires and intentions, that abstention is itself karma. The verse names the seeing that recognises both — the discriminative knowledge of the non-doing Self that grounds free action.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as resolving the confusion through the doctrine of unattached action: the karma-yogin who acts without ego (nirahaṅkāra) and without longing for fruits (niṣphala-kāma) sees that such action is, in the relevant sense, akarma — it does not bind. Conversely, the apparent abstainer whose mind is still engaged in desire-driven cognition is performing karma though appearing inactive. The seeing is recognition of agency-as-binding versus agency-as-unbinding.

disagreements.word: akarma in karma / karma in akarma

positions.rendering: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is intelligent among men
Action with realisation of non-doership is metaphysically akarma; abstention with desire is metaphysically karma
Action without ego/desire is non-binding (akarma in the relevant sense); abstention with desire is itself binding karma

disagreements.explanation: The deepest doctrinal verse in chapter 4. Shankara reads the resolution metaphysically (non-doership of the Self). Ramanuja reads it phenomenologically (action without ego-and-desire is non-binding). Both readings agree: the surface (motion vs. stillness) does not disclose the doctrinal status of the action. The verse rewards a seeing that goes beyond surface.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
यस्य सर्वे समारम्भाः कामसङ्कल्पवर्जिताः ।
ज्ञानाग्निदग्धकर्माणं तमाहुः पण्डितं बुधाः ॥ १९ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yasya sarve samārambhāḥ kāma-saṅkalpa-varjitāḥ |
jñānāgni-dagdha-karmāṇaṁ tam āhuḥ paṇḍitaṁ budhāḥ || 19 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Whose every undertaking is free from desire-driven design, whose actions are burned by the fire of knowledge — him the wise call learned (paṇḍita).
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse names the practitioner: every samārambha (undertaking) is free of kāma-saṅkalpa (desire-driven design / planning powered by craving). The result: the practitioner's actions are burned by jñānāgni — the fire of knowledge — and produce no further binding. Such a one is paṇḍita (learned) according to the budhāḥ (the wise). The verse builds on 4.18 by giving the operational signature of the seer.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as a definition: the paṇḍita is not the one with information but the one whose actions issue from knowledge — whose undertakings are uncontaminated by desire-driven design and whose karma is consumed by the fire of self-knowledge. The metaphor of fire-burning-karma is taken seriously: knowledge is what neutralises the binding force of action.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as describing the karma-yogin in whom devotion-shaped action is so settled that all undertakings are free of selfish design. Knowledge (here jñāna in the bhakti-integrated sense) becomes the fire that burns the binding-quality of past karma, so the practitioner's karmic balance is consumed. The verse links knowledge with action in the act of consuming.

positions.rendering: whose actions are burned up by the fire of perfect knowledge
actions consumed by the fire of self-knowledge — knowledge that destroys karmic binding
actions whose binding-quality is burned by knowledge integrated with devotion

disagreements.explanation: The fire-of-knowledge metaphor is preserved across all three readings. Shankara reads the fire as Self-knowledge specifically; Ramanuja reads it as bhakti-integrated knowledge; Prabhupada flattens to 'perfect knowledge.' All agree the metaphor is real: knowledge has a destructive function on accumulated karma. The verse is the chapter's introduction of this powerful image, which 4.36-4.37 will elaborate.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
त्यक्त्वा कर्मफलासङ्गं नित्यतृप्तो निराश्रयः ।
कर्मण्यभिप्रवृत्तोऽपि नैव किञ्चित्करोति सः ॥ २० ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
tyaktvā karma-phalāsaṅgaṁ nitya-tṛpto nirāśrayaḥ |
karmaṇy abhipravṛtto 'pi naiva kiñcit karoti saḥ || 20 ||

anvaya.meaning: without dependence / without shelter (in any external)

verses.literal_meaning: Having abandoned attachment to the fruits of action, ever content, dependent on nothing — though fully engaged in action, he does not act at all.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse is the operational portrait of 4.18's seer. Three qualities are named: (1) tyaktvā karma-phalāsaṅgam — having released attachment to fruits (echoing 2.47); (2) nitya-tṛpta — ever-content; (3) nirāśraya — taking shelter in nothing external. Such a one, though fully engaged in action (karmaṇy abhipravṛttaḥ), does not act at all in the binding-sense. The verse anchors the chapter's practice in coherence with the 2.47-2.48 doctrine and prepares 4.21-4.23.
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads the verse as the integrated portrait: the actor whose mind is released from fruit-attachment, whose contentment is intrinsic and constant (not contingent on outcomes), and who is dependent on no external support — such an actor's full engagement in action is, metaphysically, akarma. The verse is 4.18 made operational.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja reads the verse as the description of the karma-yogin established in the practice: the one who, having relinquished fruit-attachment, rests in the inner contentment of devotion to the Lord and does not seek refuge in any worldly support. Such a one's action, however fully performed, does not bind because it is offered, not appropriated.

positions.rendering: always engaged in work, [yet] he is doing nothing
his fully-engaged action is metaphysically akarma — non-binding because issuing from non-doership
his action does not bind because it is offered, not appropriated by an ego that claims authorship

disagreements.explanation: All three preserve the paradox: full engagement + no doing. Shankara grounds the paradox in non-doership of the Self; Ramanuja grounds it in offered action without ego-appropriation. Both readings are coherent with 2.47 (no claim to authorship of fruits) and 4.18 (the seer's paradoxical relation to action).
